Java Properties for Uniface Call-In

You can control the environment in which a Uniface component runs by setting some Java properties, either at a general package level or for sub-sets.

Property Names

A Java property has a name and a value in which both the name and the value are text strings and must be interpreted by the application. The name of a property is a series of dot-separated identifiers, allowing properties to be organized hierarchically. For example:

  • uniface.bindir
  • uniface.component.commandline
  • uniface.component.orderservice.name

The accessible properties fall into two categories:

  • General properties define something relevant to the package as a whole. For example, uniface.component.orderservice.class is a property for the component ORDERSERVICE.
  • Specialized properties apply to a subset of the package only.

    There is a wildcard feature for specialized properties if a property for a specific subset cannot be found. In this event, the configuration manager tries to find a property with the same name but with the subset asterisk (*).

All Java classes for Uniface Java call-in use the prefix uniface.component. (with the period). For example: uniface.component.product.apsname=SALES is a property for the Uniface component PRODUCT.

Setting Properties

You can set properties in several ways.

  • For the Java command line, using the -D option:

    -D PropertyName = Value

    For example:

    java -Duniface.bindir="C:/Uniface 10/common/bin"
    java -Duniface.component.average.name=CALCAVERAGE
  • Property files. On initialization, the Configuration Manager looks for a property named properties, with the correct prefix. If this property is found, it is assumed to be the name of a file containing properties. These properties do not override the current set of properties, but are added to them.

    The example shows the use of property files, where myprops.props is the property file:

    uniface.component.properties=myprops.props
 myprops.props:
 uniface.component.*.commandline=/ini=usys.ini 
 uniface.component.average.name=CALCAVERAGE
  • From within the Java program, you can use the putProp method of the ConfigManager class. However, it is not possible to set a property that has already been set. This prevents program defaults from overriding command line properties.

    For example, the following sets commandline property for all component names:

    ConfigManager.putProp (null, "commandline","/ini=c:/uniface/adm/usys.ini" + "/asn=c:/uniface/adm/usys.asn");

    For more information, see ConfigManager.putProp.

  • For properties available to the Configuration Manager, you can use the methods of the Configuration Manager JavaBean (ConfigManagerBean). For example: 

    ConfigManagerBean.setApsName("MY_APSC)

    For more information, see ConfigManagerBean.
